turns.user_message: Hi, thanks for seeing me. Yeah, I guess I'm here because I'm trying to quit smoking again. I've tried before, but it never seems to stick. I've been smoking for about 13 years now, and I know it's bad for me, but it's just so hard to quit.
step2_rag_chunks: Avoid situations where others are smoking during the early stages of quitting. Being around smokers can increase the urge to smoke.

If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.
Support from friends, family, or healthcare professionals can improve the chances of successfully quitting smoking.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the PLANNING phase. You have enough understanding of this patient. You can now gently introduce ONE relevant coping strategy or technique based on their specific triggers and past attempts. Keep it simple — one idea at a time, check if they are open to it.

turns.user_message: Well, I'm just really scared of the withdrawal symptoms. Last time I quit, I was a total nightmare to be around. I was irritable, anxious... it was like I was a different person. And the cravings were just so intense. I don't know if I can handle that again.
step2_rag_chunks: Withdrawal symptoms from nicotine may include irritability, anxiety, difficulty concentrating, increased appetite, and strong cravings.
Withdrawal symptoms usually peak within the first few days after quitting and gradually decrease over time.
Nicotine replacement therapy such as nicotine gum, patches, or lozenges can help reduce withdrawal symptoms and cravings.

step2_rag_chunks: Avoid keeping cigarettes, lighters, or ashtrays nearby when trying to quit smoking.
Nicotine addiction is both physical and psychological. Addressing both aspects can improve success in quitting.
If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.

When a craving hits, delaying the response by even 5 to 10 minutes is often enough for it to pass. Tell yourself you will smoke in 10 minutes if you still want to â€” and then do something else for those 10 minutes. Most of the time the urge has weakened significantly by the time the 10 minutes are up.
Reward yourself for milestones such as staying smoke-free for a day, a week, or a month.
